perturbation.original_text: 12. Governing Law and Jurisdiction: This agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the Province of Québec and the laws of Canada applicable to agreements made in or to be performed within such province. Each of the parties hereby submits to the jurisdiction of the courts in the Province of Québec.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Governing Law and Jurisdiction: This agreement will be governed by the laws of Canada. Each of the parties hereby submits to the jurisdiction of the courts in the Province of Québec.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the Province of Québec and the laws of Canada applicable to agreements made in or to be performed within such province," the governing law is vaguely defined. This may contradict consumer protection laws within Quebec, as it suggests only Canadian federal law applies, omitting specific provincial consumer protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Consumer Protection Act, CQLR c P-40.1
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text omits the specific application of Québec law which includes the Consumer Protection Act, CQLR c P-40.1. This Act provides various consumer protection rights and remedies that might be applicable to transactions covered by this agreement if one party is a consumer in Québec. The change suggests federal law is sufficient and intentionally ignores Quebec's consumer protection laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Privacy Act (Canada) - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Canadian federal legislation (1983) See also: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act See also: Access to Information Act Privacy Act Parliament of Canada Long title An Act to extend the present laws of Canada that protect the privacy of individuals and that provide individuals with a right of access to personal information about themselves Citation R.S.C., 1985, c. P-21 Territorial extent Canada Enacted by 32nd Canadian Parliament Commenced July 1, 1983 Administered by Privacy Commissioner of Canada Legislative history Bill title Bill C-43 Status: Current legislation The Privacy Act ( French : Loi sur la protection des renseignements personnels ) is the federal information-privacy legislation of Canada that came into effect on July 1, 1983. [ 1 ] [ 2 ] Administered by the Privacy Commissioner of Canada , [ 1 ] the Act sets out rules for how institutions of the Government of Canada collect, use, disclose, retain, and dispose of personal information of individuals . [ 3 ] The Act does not apply to political parties , political representatives (i.e., members of Parliament and senators ), courts , and private sector organizations. [ 3 ] All provinces and territories have their own laws governing their public sectors. [ 3 ] Overview [ edit ] Some salient provisions of the legislation are as follows: A government institution may not collect personal information unless it relates directly to an operating program or activity of the institution (section 4). With some exceptions, when a government institution collects an individual's personal information from the individual, it must inform the individual of the purpose for which the information is being collected (section 5(2)). With some exceptions, personal information under the control of a government institution may be used only for the purpose for which the information was obtained or for a use consistent with that purpose, unless the individual consents (section 7). With some exceptions, personal information under the control of a government institution may not be disclosed, unless the individual consents (section 8). Every Canadian citizen or permanent resident has the right to be given access to personal information about the individual under the control of a government institution that is reasonably retrievable by the government institution, and request correction if the information is inaccurate (section 12). A government institution can refuse requests for access to personal information in four cases: [ 4 ] The request interferes with the responsibilities of the government, such as national defence and law enforcement investigations (sections 19-25) The request contains the personal information of someone other than the individual who made the request (section 26). The request is subject to solicitor-client privilege (section 27). A request for an individual's own medical records can be rejected if there is no benefit to the individual in reading it (section 28). The Privacy Commissioner of Canada receives and investigates complaints, including complaints that an individual was denied access to his or her personal information held by a government institution (section 29). History [ edit ] The first privacy law in Canada was enacted in 1977 in part four of the Canadian Human Rights Act by creating the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada , which would be responsible for investigating privacy violation complaints by members of the public and reporting to lawmakers. [ 5 ] During the 32nd Parliament in 1983, Bill C-43 was passed. [ 5 ] This legislation created the Privacy Act and the Access to Information Act , separate from the Canadian Human Rights Act . Case law [ edit ] An individual who has been refused access to personal information may ultimately apply to the Federal Court for a review of the matter, pursuant to section 41 of the Act. The Court may order the
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the specific application of Québec law, including its Consumer Protection Act. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation detail how this omission removes specific provincial consumer protections. While the scraped snippets don't directly mention the Consumer Protection Act, the omission of Quebec law in its entirety when the original document explicitly included it means there is a contradiction of Quebec Law. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, where removing essential information makes the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Required Disclosure: If the Recipient or any of its Representatives is requested to disclose any Confidential Information in connection with any legal or administrative proceeding or investigation, or is required by law, regulation, stock exchange or regulatory authority to disclose any Confidential Information, such person or entity will (i) promptly notify the Corporation of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such a request or requirement (unless prohibited by law, regulation or order of a court or administrative tribunal) so that the Corporation may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy, or waive compliance with the provisions of this agreement, and (ii) if, in the absence of a protective order, such disclosure is required in the opinion of such person’s or entity’s counsel, such person or entity may make such disclosure without liability under this agreement, provided that such person or entity only furnishes that portion of the Confidential Information which is legally required, such person or entity gives the Corporation notice of the information to be disclosed as far in advance of its disclosure as practicable (unless prohibited by law, regulation or order of a court or administrative tribunal) and, upon Corporation’s request and at Corporation’s expense, such person or entity shall cooperate in any efforts by the Corporation to ensure that confidential treatment shall be accorded to such disclosed information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Required Disclosure: If the Recipient or any of its Representatives is required by law, regulation, stock exchange or regulatory authority to disclose any Confidential Information, such person or entity may make such disclosure without liability under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the requirement to notify the Corporation before disclosing confidential information when legally compelled. This contradicts obligations of good faith under Quebec law regarding contractual obligations, where parties are expected to act honestly and reasonably.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1375 of the Civil Code of Québec imposes a duty of good faith on parties in the performance of their contractual obligations. By removing the obligation to notify the Corporation of a required disclosure and cooperate in seeking a protective order, the Recipient can unilaterally disclose confidential information without considering the Corporation's interests, potentially breaching the duty of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the notification requirement directly contravenes the duty of good faith as described in the Law Explanation. The original text ensures the Corporation has an opportunity to protect its confidential information, aligning with the principle of good faith. By removing this obligation, the changed text permits unilateral disclosure, undermining the Corporation's interests and breaching the implied duty of good faith in contractual performance under Quebec law. The scraped law snippet 1 shows the Civil Code of Quebec, the legal area relevant to the law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Remedies: The Recipient understands and agrees that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this agreement by the Recipient or its Representatives and that, in addition to all other remedies, the Corporation shall be entitled to specific performance or injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Remedies: The Recipient understands and agrees that money damages shall be the only remedy for any breach of this agreement by the Recipient or its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration restricts the Corporation's remedies to only monetary damages, preventing them from seeking specific performance or injunctive relief. This may conflict with Quebec's Civil Code which allows for specific performance under certain circumstances, especially when monetary damages are insufficient.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1601 of the Civil Code of Québec allows a creditor (in this case, the Corporation) to seek specific performance of an obligation (in this case, maintaining confidentiality) if the debtor (Recipient) fails to perform it. By restricting the remedy to only monetary damages, the modified text contradicts this right to seek specific performance under Québec law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly restricts the Corporation's remedies to only monetary damages, which directly contradicts the Law Explanation which states that Article 1601 of the Civil Code of Québec allows the Corporation to seek specific performance. The original text allows for specific performance, while the changed text eliminates this option. The scraped snippet is limited, but the Law Explanation's argument is strong because the removal is a clear violation of the right to seek specific performance. Therefore, the modification creates a direct contradiction by removing a core legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Non-disclosure of Confidential Information: During the period commencing on the date of this agreement and ending on the date of termination in section 9, the Recipient will take all commercially reasonable measures to ensure the continued confidentiality of the Confidential Information and shall not disclose it to anyone except (i) to the Recipient’s Representatives under the limited terms and conditions set forth in section 3, or (ii) as permitted under section 4. Recipient further agrees not to disclose the contemplated transaction, the existence of discussions between the parties regarding the contemplated transaction or the nature or substance of those discussions to any person or entity other than the Corporation or its representatives. The Recipient shall be liable for any breaches by the Recipient’s Representatives of the provisions of this agreement dealing with restrictions on disclosure and use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Non-disclosure of Confidential Information: During the period commencing on the date of this agreement and ending on the date of termination in section 9, the Recipient shall not disclose the contemplated transaction, the existence of discussions between the parties regarding the contemplated transaction or the nature or substance of those discussions to any person or entity other than the Corporation or its representatives.
perturbation.explanation: This modification omits the core confidentiality obligation, including the requirement to take commercially reasonable measures to protect the Confidential Information. This weakens the overall protection and may violate Quebec's obligations of good faith in contractual agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the obligation to take 'all commercially reasonable measures to ensure the continued confidentiality of the Confidential Information,' the Recipient is no longer explicitly bound to act in good faith to protect the Corporation's confidential information. This omission could be interpreted as a breach of the general duty of good faith under Article 1375 of the Civil Code of Québec, as it allows the Recipient to be negligent in their handling of the information.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly requires the Recipient to take 'all commercially reasonable measures to ensure the continued confidentiality of the Confidential Information.' The changed text omits this critical obligation. The Law Explanation states that this omission 'could be interpreted as a breach of the general duty of good faith under Article 1375 of the Civil Code of Québec, as it allows the Recipient to be negligent in their handling of the information.' This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition: 'Omissions occur when a contract removes essential information, creating legal loopholes. A legal contradiction in this category happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant.' The removal of the requirement to take commercially reasonable measures directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith, a crucial element in Quebec contract law as stated in the Law Explanation.
